LESSONS_ADDED:
  🟤 Architect-Execute model: Sonnet subagents in this project inherit SessionStart hooks that auto-load CLAUDE.md + .claude/rules/* — burns Sonnet's 60K window before any task budget. Default to Opus self-executor (Step 2.5b) for this project until subagent context can be trimmed or hooks made conditional.
  🟤 Edge-safe vs DB-revalidating session callback duplication: auth.config.ts (Edge) AND auth.ts (Node) both have session callbacks that copy JWT claims into session.user. New JWT fields MUST be wired in BOTH. The DB-revalidating variant in auth.ts can additionally pull fresh DB values for fields where token-staleness matters (e.g., org slug renames).
  🟤 Super-admin tenant URL policy = option C (path-scoped bypass): /superadmin/* paths bypass the URL↔session slug check; all other paths enforce the match even for super-admins (redirected to their own org subdomain). Codified in resolveTenantRedirect.
  ⚖️ vercel-plugin nextjs skill auto-recommends proxy.ts (Next.js 16) on every middleware.ts touch. Yelli is on Next.js 15.5.18 — recommendation is irrelevant until a separate Next.js 16 upgrade ticket. Future agents touching middleware.ts: ignore the proxy.ts recommendation block.

PHASE_7_TICKET_7_NOTES:
  - Why JWT-encoded slug (not DB lookup per request): the slug check runs on EVERY Edge request. A DB round-trip per request would dominate p99 latency and tie the Edge bundle to platformPrisma (which it currently avoids — see auth.config.ts top comment). Encoding the slug in the JWT moves the check to a string comparison. Freshness on org slug rename is provided by the Node-side session callback in auth.ts which re-fetches `current.organization.slug` on every session read; the Edge variant in auth.config.ts trusts the token value (rotation handles staleness).
  - Why super-admin option C (not A "always allow" or B "apex only"): option A would silently let super-admins view any tenant's UI without an explicit consent surface, making cross-tenant data exfiltration via super-admin compromise harder to audit. Option B would mean super-admins can't ever debug a tenant in its own URL context (annoying ops UX). Option C threads the needle: /superadmin/* is the only path where slug doesn't matter (platform administration UI), all other paths enforce match. If a super-admin wants to view tenant acme's UI, they explicitly switch their session's organizationId to acme via a platform action (which the session re-validation in auth.ts catches on next session read via security_version). The /superadminer/foo guard test asserts the prefix match is exact (`/superadmin` or `/superadmin/`), not a string startsWith — prevents future routes like /superadminer from accidentally inheriting the bypass.
  - Why the pure-helper extraction: middleware.ts imports `next-auth` and `next/server`, both Edge-runtime modules. When vitest tries to resolve middleware.ts in Node, `next-auth/lib/env.js` fails to import `next/server`. Pure functions with no runtime-specific imports (just `string`, `URL`, regex) can be co-located under server/ alongside auth.config.ts (which is also Edge-runtime-safe). This gives clean Node-importable tests without a refactor of middleware.ts itself.
  - Why drop the defensive `if (session.user.organizationId)` guard: in c-1, organizationId became required-or-undefined-user via the invalid predicate. If `session.user` exists, both organizationId and organizationSlug are guaranteed strings by TypeScript. Defensive null-checks at that point are dead code that masks real type changes.
